Hello, sports fans, and welcome back to Wall Power, my four-times-per-week private email on the inner workings of the art market\xe2\x80\x94which includes a bonus Inner Circle issue every Wednesday for hard core professionals, ruthless advisors, billionaire (and aspiring billionaire) collectors, and true believers. I\xe2\x80\x99m Marion Maneker.

Mar 04, 2025

Marion Maneker

Hello, sports fans, and welcome back to Wall Power, my four-times-per-week private email on the inner workings of the art market\xe2\x80\x94which includes a bonus Inner Circle issue every Wednesday for hard core professionals, ruthless advisors, billionaire (and aspiring billionaire) collectors, and true believers. I\xe2\x80\x99m Marion Maneker .

Tonight, Julie Davich is going deep on the Saunders collection, which was announced late last week by Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s. Estimated at $80 million, this will be the most valuable single-owner sale of Old Masters paintings to be offered ever. Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s is sufficiently pumped about this collection to have moved it to May, ahead of the normal schedule.

There\xe2\x80\x99s an added benefit to scheduling the sale then. The preview and sale will allow collectors of modern and contemporary art\xe2\x80\x94and their advisors\xe2\x80\x94to see the works when they are in town. Consider the Saunders sale as lead generation for the Old Masters category. As Julie and I have been telling you, collectors have become interested in Old Masters for the high quality of the art and the lower prices\xe2\x80\x94in relative terms, of course. Julie gets into all of that below.

But first\xe2\x80\xa6

  • A new front in the battle for Artnet : Late last week, as I recounted, Artnet selected a neutral board of directors at its contentious annual general meeting, but that doesn\xe2\x80\x99t mean the warring factions have made peace. Over the weekend, the company released a statement, signed by V.P. Sophie Neuendorf , still obsessing over the company\xe2\x80\x99s largest shareholder, Neuendorf nemesis R\xc3\xbcdiger Weng \xe2\x80\x94even though by then, Weng had been outmaneuvered by offstage investor Andrew Wolff . \xe2\x80\x9cThe meeting was at times turbulent,\xe2\x80\x9d the weekend statement reads, \xe2\x80\x9cas one or more shareholders attempted to push their personal interests with the help of professional disruptors.\xe2\x80\x9d

    Presumably, Sophie is referring to the conspiracy theory that Weng had hired a disruptive shareholder to prolong the meeting. Curiously, for what it\xe2\x80\x99s worth, another neutral attendee speculated that the raucous 14-hour meeting had been staged by the Neuendorfs themselves, in a desperate attempt to stall the proceedings until a white knight emerged in the form of Wolff.

    Perhaps not surprisingly, there\xe2\x80\x99s a new showdown brewing, after Wolff seemed to be engineering a takeunder, wherein the stock price plummets to facilitate a takeover. There\xe2\x80\x99s also been talk of the company needing to borrow another $1 million from newly elected board member Roy Israel , just to make payroll in the coming months. Meanwhile, a San Francisco tech turnaround investor named Garry McGuire \xe2\x80\x94who unexpectedly announced at the board meeting that he planned to make a bid\xe2\x80\x94has maneuvered himself closer to center stage in the melodramatic battle.

    Anyway, the Neuendorfs believe that Weng reneged on a previously agreed-on truce and sabotaged the company\xe2\x80\x99s future by denying them the ability to raise capital. \xe2\x80\x9cMr. Weng inexplicably failed to adhere to the agreement on the Supervisory Board election,\xe2\x80\x9d the statement continued tartly. \xe2\x80\x9cBy doing so, he has once again prioritized his personal interests over those of the company\xe2\x80\x94despite the fact that the art market has been in a severe crisis for years.\xe2\x80\x9d

    Who betrayed whom? We\xe2\x80\x99ll probably never know, but the damage has been done. Artnet\xe2\x80\x99s stock price dropped by as much as 25 percent during the four days following the meeting. Meanwhile, Wolff\xe2\x80\x99s takeunder plans\xe2\x80\x94which would eventually involve rolling Artnet up with other similar digital art businesses\xe2\x80\x94seemed to be proceeding apace until today\xe2\x80\xa6 when Weng announced that he would pledge his 29.5 percent stake in Artnet to Art Technology Holdings, a company McGuire formed less than a year ago. The partnership would allow McGuire to make his own bid to acquire Artnet at \xe2\x82\xac11 a share, which was nearly twice the stock price on Monday. (Artnet shares are now trading in the middle ground of \xe2\x82\xac7 a share, as investors weigh which way to go.)

    Unlike Wolff\xe2\x80\x99s planned roll-up, ATH believes that it can invest up to $10 million in the company, install experienced professional management instead of family members, and add value across the art market value chain. I haven\xe2\x80\x99t made a secret of my skepticism. Despite its traffic and brand, I worry that there isn\xe2\x80\x99t a large enough addressable market for a revamped and fully functioning Artnet to grow into the valuation worthy of an acquisition and subsequent investment. But McGuire is a good tech salesman. Which means the battle for Artnet has suddenly shifted: It\xe2\x80\x99s no longer a German civil war, but a struggle between Silicon Valley and Wall Street.

Now, let\xe2\x80\x99s get to the main event \xe2\x80\xa6

Saunders Spring

Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s has set its most valuable Old Masters auction ever for May: the Saunders collection, the product of a four-year buying spree fueled by Wall Street money, which could fetch more than $80 million.

Julie Brener Davich

\xe2\x80\x9cOld Masters lovers share several traits: curiosity, passion for provenance, love of color and history,\xe2\x80\x9d wrote collector Jordan Saunders , who amassed one of the most consequential collections of such paintings in the 20th century along with her late husband, the legendary Morgan Stanley banker and private equity O.G. Thomas Saunders III . \xe2\x80\x9cI am in awe of the artists\xe2\x80\x99 techniques, the stories they tell, all the tiny details, and, in some, the hidden symbolism.\xe2\x80\x9d

This May, the Saunders collection will go on sale at Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s, in New York, with an estimate of $80 million\xe2\x80\x94the most valuable single-owner compilation ever to hit the block within the category. \xe2\x80\x9cThere are so many high-quality, fabulous-condition pictures in the sale, just one after the other,\xe2\x80\x9d said George Wachter , a Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s chairman and co-worldwide-head of Old Masters paintings\xe2\x80\x94so many, in fact, that Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s felt it could sustain a single-owner event outside the regular Old Masters marquee sale calendar. Wachter had to alert his clients to add it to their travel schedules. (Disclosure: I worked on the Saunders collection proposal when I was at Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s.)

The timing is fortuitous. May is also when all the modern and contemporary collectors are in town, and art advisors have been pitching their clients on the value of Old Masters. Evidence from London this week\xe2\x80\x94where Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s displayed highlights from the Saunders collection alongside the presale viewing of modern and contemporary art\xe2\x80\x94suggests they\xe2\x80\x99re onto something. A Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s spokesman told me that a well-known contemporary artist happened upon the Saunders trove and \xe2\x80\x9cgot really jazzed,\xe2\x80\x9d attracting a crowd around her as she waxed eloquent about the brushwork.

The Saunders\xe2\x80\x99 New York apartment. Photo: Courtesy of Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s

The couple\xe2\x80\x99s collection spans the 16th to 19th centuries, and represents some of the greatest artists of that era, ranging from still lifes by Jan Davidsz. de Heem and Luis Mel\xc3\xa9ndez to portraits by Frans Hals and Thomas Lawrence to vedute by Canaletto and Francesco Guardi , as well as works by Gerrit Dou and Peter Paul Rubens . The auction comprises 56 works, with 42 being offered in a dedicated single-owner sale and the rest in the various-owners sale the following day. By comparison, Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s most valuable collection of Old Masters sold to date was the Fisch Davidson collection, which made $76 million in 2023\xe2\x80\x94it was only 12 pictures, all Italian Baroque, with two-thirds of the value coming from just two Rubens paintings.


This will be the first time in decades that the Saunders paintings are coming on the market, since the couple bought many of them via private transactions between 1998 and 2002. \xe2\x80\x9cThe Saunderses wanted beautiful pictures in wonderful condition,\xe2\x80\x9d said Wachter, who worked closely with the couple to assemble the collection that would fill their triplex penthouse at 812 Park Avenue. \xe2\x80\x9cWhat\xe2\x80\x99s amazing is how I managed to find all these things at that time. Not sure I could do it in the same way today,\xe2\x80\x9d he told me, in part because Old Masters collections like those of Renata and Michal Hornstein , Rose-Marie and Eijk van Otterloo , and Susan and Matthew Weatherbie have been donated to museums.

Full Circle

The Saunders\xe2\x80\x99 collecting journey is ending right where it started. In January 1998, Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s client services executive Mish Tworkowski invited Jordan to lunch at the auction house, where they wandered down to the Old Masters exhibition and happened upon the painting View of the Isola della Giudecca , by Guardi. Tworkowski introduced her to Wachter, who spoke eloquently about the composition. The Saunderses went on to purchase the Guardi at auction for $1.3 million. (It\xe2\x80\x99s being offered in May at an estimate of $4 million.) In the same sale, they underbid a portrait by Johannes Cornelisz Verspronck that ended up being bought by the National Gallery for $1.65 million.

Thus began their four-year buying spree and decades-long friendship with Wachter\xe2\x80\x94the beau ideal of how auction house marketing is supposed to work. He advised them in putting together their collection at a time when auction houses specialists still played that role, traveling with them to Rome and Montreal to view pictures, sometimes accompanied by their interior designer, Ina Lindemann , whom Wachter would later hire to decorate his own home. He brokered the Saunders\xe2\x80\x99 private purchase of eight exceptional Old Masters works from the Hornsteins, including the Dou, Lawrence, and Mel\xc3\xa9ndez.

It was also Wachter who steered them to a landscape painting by Frans Post that had been discovered in a barn in Connecticut. The painting, which they found out had belonged to Louis XIV , was filthy. But the Saunderses paid $2.2 million for it on Wachter\xe2\x80\x99s advice and sent it to a conservator, who started by cleaning a small square of the sky, uncovering a brilliant blue, at which point the couple knew they had a masterpiece. Wachter advised them to copy an elaborate frame from the Rijksmuseum, which took a Dutch artisan a year to replicate. The painting, in that frame, is now being offered at Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s at an estimate of $6 million. (The artist\xe2\x80\x99s current record, set in 1997, is $4.5 million.)


The auction also includes a pair of Guardi paintings that the couple acquired at Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s in 2000 for $5.6 million and are now being offered for $10 million. (In that same auction they underbid Rubens\xe2\x80\x99s Portrait of a Man as Mars , which would eventually end up in the Fisch Davidson collection.) Their primary competitor for the Guardis happened to be at lunch and unreachable when the lot came up at auction, so they got the painting for far less than they might have otherwise.

The Saunders collection on view at the Virginia Museum of Fine Arts. Photo: Sandra Sellars

Thomas Saunders, who died in 2022, was a managing director and chairman at Morgan Stanley for two decades\xe2\x80\x94where he structured I.P.O.s for companies like Apple and GE, and did the underwriting for Margaret Thatcher \xe2\x80\x99s telecom privatization\xe2\x80\x94before going on to co-found his own private equity firm. He also served as chairman of the Heritage Foundation, which of course famously authored Project 2025. The couple were active philanthropists, with much of their largesse going to Virginia institutions such as UVA, the Virginia Military Institute, and the Thomas Jefferson Foundation.

Jordan first tested the auction waters by offering English and European furniture, silver, ceramics, and Chinese export porcelain in a single-owner sale at Sotheby\xe2\x80\x99s in February 2024, including items she\xe2\x80\x99d purchased in the Duke and Duchess of Windsor sale there in 1998. The sale of 316 objects made $3.3 million, against an estimate of $2 million.

Additional highlights in the upcoming sale include a de Heem large-scale floral still life at an estimate of $8 million, higher than the artist\xe2\x80\x99s current auction record of $7.6 million. Mel\xc3\xa9ndez\xe2\x80\x99s Still Life with a Cauliflower is estimated at $5 million, and Lawrence\xe2\x80\x99s Portrait of Miss Julia Peel is estimated at $6 million. It is the best example by the artist to appear at auction in nearly two decades, when the auction record of $4 million was set. A rare pair of Hals portraits is on offer for $6 million, and a genre scene by Dou, a student of Rembrandt , is estimated at $5 million. Thomas recalled seeing the Dou painting on view in a 2001 traveling exhibition of the artist\xe2\x80\x99s works: \xe2\x80\x9cWhat can I say about my feelings as I looked at our Dou hanging in the National Gallery in Washington next to Queen Elizabeth II \xe2\x80\x99s Dou? God bless America!\xe2\x80\x9d

After London, the paintings are touring to Amsterdam and Brussels during TEFAF Maastricht, then Los Angeles and Hong Kong in April. As Jordan would tell the next generation of collectors, \xe2\x80\x9cLong live the hunt!\xe2\x80\x9d

Endnotes\xe2\x80\xa6

A reader teased me about Julie\xe2\x80\x99s coverage of Nino Mier Gallery\xe2\x80\x99s show of Deborah Druick paintings in Sunday\xe2\x80\x99s email. ( Mier ran into difficulties last year when it was reported that he had been underpaying his own artists.) That\xe2\x80\x99s fine. If we refused to write about any dealer who had run into some difficulties or even, possibly , behaved badly, we would have no one to write about at all. But that interaction started a conversation. (By the way, the reader did not realize that when you reply to one of these newsletters, it does indeed go straight to my pocket\xe2\x80\x94and I\xe2\x80\x99ll write you back!)

The reader also suggested that Puck allow comments for \xe2\x80\x9cinteraction and engagement,\xe2\x80\x9d figuring it would do well, \xe2\x80\x9csince everyone I talk to about the art world wants to know what people are thinking and doing and why, etcetera.\xe2\x80\x9d These are all good points. But they also illustrate what makes Puck unique. So I thought I would take a moment to explain what we\xe2\x80\x99re trying to do here and how it benefits you.

First, we want your feedback\xe2\x80\x94good or bad. Please feel free to object, correct, or simply add to our coverage. We strive to tell you what\xe2\x80\x99s happening and why. But we cannot do it alone. In fact, a big part of what we\xe2\x80\x99re doing here is reflecting back to you what we hear from you and others. So, please hit reply. Or text me at 917.825.1391. That number is also good on Signal and WhatsApp. My email is indeed Marion@puck.news . Hit me up. The same goes for Julie, who can be reached at Jdavich@puck.news .

In short: The more you respond to us, the more we can present those comments here in the newsletter. Anonymously, of course. It\xe2\x80\x99s our job to fairly represent your thinking\xe2\x80\x94even when it conflicts with or contradicts our own. A good contest of ideas is how we all shape and sharpen our thinking. That\xe2\x80\x99s also how we engage our readers and let them know what people are thinking and doing, and why.

Hope to hear from you,

M

Puck founding partner Matt Belloni takes you inside the business of Hollywood, using exclusive reporting and insight to explain the backstories on everything from Marvel movies to the streaming wars.

The ultimate fashion industry bible, offering incisive reportage on all aspects of the business and its biggest players. Anchored by preeminent fashion journalist Lauren Sherman, Line Sheet also features veteran reporter Rachel Strugatz, who delivers unparalleled intel on what\xe2\x80\x99s happening in the beauty industry, and Sarah Shapiro, a longtime retail strategist who writes about e-commerce, brick-and-mortar, D.T.C., and more.

Oscar Dishonors

MATTHEW BELLONI

Zelensky\xe2\x80\x99s Unforced Error

JULIA IOFFE
